model_narrative: We run a transportation operation that ships a product from a set of plants to a set of markets. Each plant has a limited amount it can produce, and each market has a requirement it would like met. Total demand exceeds total supply, so the markets cannot all be fully served. For each market we decide how much to ship in from each plant, and we also allow each market's requirement to be relaxed by some amount so that a feasible plan exists. The objective is to make the total amount of relaxation granted across all markets as small as possible, finding the least shortfall that restores feasibility.

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the canning plants, which act as the supply nodes that ship product out

sets.name: j
sets.doc: the markets, which act as the demand nodes that need product delivered

params.name: a
params.doc: the production capacity available at each plant, in cases

params.name: b
params.doc: the requirement that must be met at each market, in cases, already inflated by twenty percent above the nominal level

params.name: c
params.doc: the per-case transport cost from a plant to a market, in thousands of dollars per case

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the quantity shipped from a plant to a market, in cases

vars.name: r
vars.doc: the amount by which a market's requirement is allowed to fall short, that is the relaxation or slack granted to that market's demand, in cases

objective.expr_var: total demand relaxation summed over all markets

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: Each plant can only ship out as much as it is able to produce. For each plant, the total quantity shipped from that plant to all of the markets must not exceed that plant's available capacity.

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: Each market's requirement has to be covered, but we permit it to be met short by granting that market some relaxation. For each market, the total quantity shipped into it from all of the plants, together with the relaxation granted to that market, must be at least that market's requirement.

query: Maintenance is planning to throttle seattle's outbound dock for a stretch — total cases leaving seattle would be held to 300. Where does our total shortfall land if that happens?
expected_pyomo: model.seattle_outbound = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x['seattle', j] for j in model.j) <= 300)

query: Logistics wants to stress-test capping san-diego's total outbound shipments at 500 cases. What does that do to the amount of demand we can't cover?
expected_pyomo: model.sd_outbound = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x['san-diego', j] for j in model.j) <= 500)

query: There's talk of a network-wide throughput limit during the rail slowdown — no more than 900 cases moving across all lanes combined. How much demand goes unmet under that ceiling?
expected_pyomo: model.total_throughput = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[i, j] for i in model.i for j in model.j) <= 900)

query: The plan runs san-diego flat out at 600 cases — that's an awful lot of load on one plant. Shouldn't we hold its total outbound under 450? What's the optimizer seeing that makes it lean on san-diego that hard?
expected_pyomo: model.sd_total_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x['san-diego', j] for j in model.j) <= 450)

query: Seattle is pinned at its full 350 in this plan. Running it wide open doesn't sit right with me — couldn't we keep its total shipments under 250 and let the slack go elsewhere? What's the model defending by maxing it out?
expected_pyomo: model.sea_total_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x['seattle', j] for j in model.j) <= 250)
